words: OTTO SCHUPFER:— I started in 1921 a little place down here but
you couldn't make it go then finally combined the two and started
in Kendrick over. Well, not '21- wait a minute, I'm wrong on that
Started in '21- well, we didn't start that until '21-
space went between there I don't remember.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of equipment did you have in '21?

speaker: OS
words: Powers, same as all of 'em. Troy had the same thing and it was all

speaker: OS
words: silent then.

speaker: SS
words: But it wasn't a going proposition in Juliaetta?

speaker: OS
words: No, no, it never was a going proposition by itself anyway. Down

speaker: OS
words: here we took in $25 and by the time you give the musicians about five

speaker: OS
words: of it and about $15 for the picture, and you can see about how it go.

speaker: OS
words: Just run it more for the fun of it than anything else. And in Ken

speaker: OS
words: drick it was about the same thing. It was never a money making pro

speaker: OS
words: position. But I figured out for one year we made twenty-five cents

speaker: OS
words: an hour, the four of us working there. And Harmon he bought the pic

speaker: OS
words: tures. But still it helout

speaker: OS
words: ped me and helped people make a living. And it was work, it wasn't

speaker: OS
words: doing other things, just night work, you might say. And them days

speaker: OS
words: people wasn't afraid to put in an extra- a little extra time. But

speaker: OS
words: now, you have to have time and a half for that.

speaker: SS
words: How often did you show a movie? Every night or just on a weekend?

speaker: OS
words: Pretty near every Friday and Saturday unless it was a Fourth of July

speaker: OS
words: or some celebration, why we put in some extra ones. There for a lit

speaker: OS
words: tle while we run four a week, but that didn't work out either.

speaker: SS
words: Now about when did you think that the Kendrick theatre started?

speaker: OS
words: I think in 1929 or '30- It had started in 1918 by somebody else,

speaker: OS
words: run if there the fella that had the newspaper. It was

speaker: OS
words: started by Oldfield.

speaker: SS
words: Oldfield was the first?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah. Of course there was a theatre before, only this Oldfield bought

speaker: OS
words: the jewelry store in Kendrick and he had the theatre and he moved it

speaker: OS
words: down Pullman. And he run it for a little while but he tried to make

speaker: OS
words: a living without doing anything else and that didn't work. Then

speaker: OS
words: Mc Pherson, the printer, he run it. He did about the same thing

speaker: OS
words: just made another fifty cents that evening, why, that was fifty cents

speaker: OS
words: more. He was working his head off during the day times, that was his

speaker: OS
words: job, and the show was something extra, him and his wife.

speaker: SS
words: What were you charging admission?

speaker: OS
words: Well, we charged all from two bits and ten up to seventy-five cents,

speaker: OS
words: I guess, about the most. Most of the time it was about fifty

speaker: OS
words: cents or twenty-five cents. Fourth of July we always used to have

speaker: OS
words: a house full. Never charged over twenty-five cents then.

speaker: SS
words: What about this orchestra? You had to hire musicians?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, there was some musicians down here, because the barber's wife

speaker: OS
words: was a pianist and another fella he played the saxophone and they

speaker: OS
words: generally furnished the music. And the way runs; for a long while

speaker: OS
words: if we took in $20 we given them about half of it and we took the

speaker: OS
words: other half. If you didn't make nothin' they got nothin'i

speaker: SS
words: Well, how many people, say in Juliaetta, how many would show up to

speaker: SS
words: see the movie in a week?

speaker: OS
words: Sometimes thirty or forty, plus the kids.

speaker: SS
words: Would you say that that was the main entertainment for the town?

speaker: SS
words: At the time, or were there other things that were more popular?

speaker: OS
words: There wasn't nothing here, there wasn't even a beer parlor then.

speaker: OS
words: Because there was a show that was down here before- oh, a fella come

speaker: OS
words: in here with it and he tried to make a go and he made a failure of

speaker: OS
words: it. Richardson, down here, he bought the thing, but he couldn't

speaker: OS
words: run it himself and he had to hire some so that didn't work at all.

speaker: OS
words: And then I think I bought the piano, the whole thing and

speaker: OS
words: with one projector. Then we kept running it every Friday and Saturday

speaker: OS
words: for a while. it got'a little bit tougher all the time, my bro

speaker: OS
words: ther he said, "Let's get some of the higher priced shows, see if that

speaker: OS
words: will help." So we got four of 'em- three of 'em the highest priced

speaker: OS
words: we could get, had to pay $25 a piece plus transportation. Among

speaker: OS
words: them was Charlie Kotft , you've probably heard of it and UP

speaker: OS
words: in Mabel's Room, and the Volga Boatmen and on those we come out- I

speaker: OS
words: think we made on the four of 'em probably $10- the two of us. Those

speaker: OS
words: were the last ones we run, then we cut down and we finally got hold

speaker: OS
words: of the Kendrick.

speaker: SS
words: The Volga Boatmen? Was that the name of it?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Volga?

speaker: OS
words: Yes, Russian picture. Volga River's in Russia. It was a darn good

speaker: OS
words: show.

speaker: SS
words: So they didn't make much more money than the others did? Or did

speaker: SS
words: they?

speaker: SS
words: Well, why did you go to Kendrick? Did you think there would be a

speaker: SS
words: better market up there, or what?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, we had the telephone outfit and we owned- it finally got so I

speaker: OS
words: think I loaned a fellow that owned the building a fair amount of

speaker: OS
words: money on it, and we got it and that's how we got the blamed thing.

speaker: OS
words: Oh, yes, they was a little bit better

speaker: OS
words: Well, it was a stock company and quit. Mc Pherson couldn't make

speaker: OS
words: it any more. He was paying I think $20 a month rent and he couldn't

speaker: OS
words: make a go of it any more, so he'quit,a fellow name of Zale bought it.

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, he bought it, but see on the first year's work he was going

speaker: OS
words: to fix up the building. Well, he tore fiown the building more than

speaker: OS
words: anything else and run a year for nothin'. Then we finally started

speaker: OS
words: in with it.

speaker: OS
words: Did you let the Juliaetta one go then? Did you stop?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, we moved the- then we were going to have two machines in there

speaker: OS
words: after , then we could run a continuous show, before you had to

speaker: OS
words: stop. There used to be a fellow name of Akenbottom used to run one

speaker: OS
words: down here before I did. And I think he charged ten and twenty-five

speaker: OS
words: cents. And he and the light company couldn't get along, so he coul

speaker: OS
words: dn't use electricity, so he had to use carbide light and that's very

speaker: OS
words: poor lights, but he had a few customers.

speaker: OS
words: Was it in the same building as yours?

speaker: OS
words: it was, but he finally they kept amoving around.

speaker: OS
words: Well, did business pick up when you went to Kendrick?

speaker: OS
words: What did you say?

speaker: OS
words: Did the business pick up, the movie business?

speaker: OS
words: About the way it was. in "Kendrick before. See, it wasn't shut down.

speaker: OS
words: When had it, he put in the first talking picture and he did pret

speaker: OS
words: ty good there for a while, but then when he quit, why, we just kept

speaker: OS
words: on agoing until after he quit. We made a little money on it all the

speaker: OS
words: time. But if you made two bits an hour; there was four of us, that

speaker: OS
words: was about an hour and five or six hours a day, why, it was better than

speaker: OS
words: just doing nothin'.

speaker: OS
words: Did the audiences get bigger once the talkies came in?

speaker: OS
words: We didn't have it just when they come in, but Sell it. They had a

speaker: OS
words: good crowd for the first two or three shows, but then after that it

speaker: OS
words: kind of got back to where it was again.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it made any difference in the movies? Were they better

speaker: SS
words: when they started having talkies, or do you think they went downhill?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, the pictures was about the same. Of course, it was a little bit

speaker: OS
words: easier to understand what you was doing about then than it was before.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't have to pay the orchestra though when you had sound, did

speaker: SS
words: you?

speaker: OS
words: No. Didn't have to do that any more. But we run it quite a while in

speaker: OS
words: Kendrick without sound. Sell- he quit why, we kept sound from

speaker: OS
words: then on. See, sound come in about 1927. We didn't have them to pay.

speaker: OS
words: We used to get the pictures from Spokane that cost about five or six

speaker: OS
words: dollars- that much for both ways, but.it cost about thirty. And you

speaker: OS
words: to send them by parcel post and then they come so high and the

speaker: OS
words: railroad would compete with them, they'd bring 'em one way that you

speaker: OS
words: had to pay for and the other way half price. Well, that worked the

speaker: OS
words: express company they finally cancelled that. And then, by golly, it

speaker: OS
words: finally went to parcel post again, that went up like everything and

speaker: OS
words: then it finally come to- now it's Greyhound. They cost about $25

speaker: OS
words: each way.

speaker: SS
words: You know, I've heard that local girls would want to become movie ac

speaker: SS
words: tresses after they'd see those films.

speaker: OS
words: I don't know whether they had any of that here or not. Yeah we had

speaker: OS
words: a few pretty good ones- fellow name of Fred Thompson and Westerly

speaker: OS
words: he made good. And of course, that's when we had the silent

speaker: OS
words: down here. Then when the talking Old Gene Autry and Roy Rogers-

speaker: OS
words: Shirley Temple was a blamed good one. And the best money we took

speaker: OS
words: in a picture was- oh, what was the name?(Josephine, what was the name

speaker: OS
words: of that picture we took in the most money in Kendrick?)

speaker: OS
words: MRS S:   What was that?

speaker: OS
words: What was the name of that picture down in Kendrick we took in and

speaker: OS
words: and the biggest crowd one time?

speaker: OS
words: MRS S:   Trail of the Lonesome Pine, wasn't it?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, Trail of the Lonesome Pine.

speaker: SS
words: When was that?

speaker: OS
words: We run that in Kendrick.

speaker: OS
words: Mrs.S: We took in over $300- that was an awful lot of money.

speaker: SS
words: Over $300 in admission?

speaker: SS
words: Mrs.S: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: One night?

speaker: SS
words: Mrs. S: And we only-

speaker: OS
words: We run it three nights.

speaker: OS
words: Mrs. S: Well, we run it until one o'clock; see the theatre was small, we

speaker: OS
words: wanted the people to see the picture and it was our fault if they

speaker: OS
words: couldn't get in, see, so we run it 'til one o'clock. We run it,

speaker: OS
words: some of iti three times.

speaker: SS
words: So, was this what you took in one night, was $300.00?

speaker: OS
words: That was the three nights.

speaker: SS
words: How many could you put in the movie theatre?

speaker: SS
words: Mrs. S: There were two hundred and forty some seats.

speaker: SS
words: 240 seats.

speaker: OS
words: Somewhere's around there. We took the front ones out

speaker: OS
words: throwing stuff at the screen

speaker: OS
words: they kept and the further they'd throw it, why, the better it was.

speaker: OS
words: Kept taking the front ones out all the time.

speaker: SS
words: What's the idea of throwing stuff at the screen?

speaker: OS
words: They wasn't as bad here as over Nez Perce, they got in there with

speaker: OS
words: air rifles. When they'd have the Indians fighting over there they'd

speaker: OS
words: shoot at 'em with air rifles and punch holes in the screen. We've got

speaker: OS
words: two or three holes- oh, we had the screen quite a little while about

speaker: OS
words: a $250 screen and it wasn't very long 'til there was a hole or two

speaker: OS
words: in it. Then we got another big nuisance, they come in with these wa

speaker: OS
words: ter pistols, and a lot of people wouldn't come to a show anymore.

speaker: SS
words: Why would— they squirted people in the audience?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, yes, they'd keep a squirting all over. They do that everywheres

speaker: OS
words: as far as that goes.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when did you stop showing movies in Kendrick?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, right after I had the stroke. See, I used to run a machine 'til

speaker: OS
words: Jerry Brown left - run the last show, it was '72 I think. Walt Dis

speaker: OS
words: ney was the last picture we run.

speaker: SS
words: Were you showing it weekly until then? Or just once in a while?

speaker: OS
words: Once in a while we run them. Whenever we got a good picture come up

speaker: OS
words: we thought maybe we would run it. Mary Poppins, was the last show we

speaker: OS
words: run. And then we had this fellow Jerry Brown, - he liked to monkey

speaker: OS
words: with stuff.A I'd run it on Friday and he'd run it on Saturday. And,

speaker: OS
words: oh, he didn't think I was running it good enough and he'd run it on

speaker: OS
words: both nights. And we generally paid him about two dollars, maybe four

speaker: OS
words: or five He's the same guy- on the telephone outfit

speaker: OS
words: he wanted- he was, oh, a good mechanic. He wanted to the

speaker: OS
words: telephone outfit and he got to learn the blamed business real good.

speaker: OS
words: And, by golly, on the switchboard stuff, he was right there putting

speaker: OS
words: stuff in, get the blueprints out and study 'em. When they put the

speaker: OS
words: dial system in Kendrick, and far was the two main guys- well,

speaker: OS
words: they did all the work to put it in. Didn't have electric man- this

speaker: OS
words: fellow that sold the equipment, well, he said,"Well, install

speaker: OS
words: it." So, we got two of 'em and they did a blamed good job. And then

speaker: OS
words: Brown, when finally he- he never made very much, he couldn't pay two

speaker: OS
words: guys, but he'd go along anyway and see what he could learn. And

speaker: OS
words: finally when this man in Troy died, had a telephone outfit, why, he

speaker: OS
words: took over and he ' a blamed good job up there by him not always

speaker: OS
words: asking, "When do I get a raise in pay?" He never asked about that

speaker: OS
words: at all. But we always give him one. Now, he's got a good job up

speaker: OS
words: there; he's general manager at Troy. But he was quite a genius in

speaker: OS
words: the electric . Well, this public address system in Kendrickthat's

speaker: OS
words: one of his. About the first thing he did, he built a public

speaker: OS
words: amplifier and he took a blueprint from the theatre amplifier, made

speaker: OS
words: an amplifier.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think- do you think that for a guy like that it's just

speaker: SS
words: natural talent that he had? Born with it?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, natural talent and then he wasn't a fellow that was always a

speaker: OS
words: howling, how much money to get, and when do they get their first vaca

speaker: OS
words: tion. Well, he was that kind of a fellow lots of times he got nothing-

speaker: OS
words: if we paid him. . Course, that isn't the

speaker: OS
words: union way of doing it. Supposed to pay him big wages all the time

speaker: OS
words: and when you don't need 'em lay 'em off and stuff like that. We

speaker: OS
words: never did that.A But he was a blamed good guy.

speaker: SS
words: You know, I was going to ask you about like- on the telephone and on

speaker: SS
words: your electric line- some of that repair work- you'd have to do line

speaker: SS
words: work- like in the bad weather in the winter. That must have been

speaker: SS
words: pretty rugged.

speaker: OS
words: Oh, yes, it was sometimes. There was one Easter Sunday I was up on

speaker: OS
words: American RidgeK Easter dinner and I got a telephone saying I had to

speaker: OS
words: come right down and there was twenty-three poles down between here

speaker: OS
words: and Kendrick.(conversation between Mrs. Schupfer and Mr. Schupfer)

speaker: SS
words: Why were twenty-six poles down?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, that was in 1919, I think. The snow got that big around

speaker: OS
words: it was wet, thirty-five wires in Kendrick and that got too heavy for

speaker: OS
words: it. I've got a picture of it here somewhere, maybe showed it to you

speaker: OS
words: the other day. One of 'em turned clear over. We had 'em all working

speaker: OS
words: by night again. We got some of these local guys around here and we

speaker: OS
words: had some pretty good neighbors around here.

speaker: SS
words: Now, was it snow?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, wet snow. Whenever it rains then it starts in asnowin' and

speaker: OS
words: that hangs on, and the wires keep agroin'. And there was another

speaker: OS
words: time we had a lot of trouble about the same thing in Kendrick, about

speaker: OS
words: four, five poles went down and that was in 1937, I guess. And the

speaker: OS
words: same time was just building their line, by golly, they just

speaker: OS
words: couldn't keep their lights on at all for a while. The wires would

speaker: OS
words: build up great big and then the snow'd melt off of the bottom one

speaker: OS
words: and up to the top one, up to the light. But they'd never had one quite

speaker: OS
words: as bad as that since that.

speaker: SS
words: When you say all these poles went down; they didn't fall down did

speaker: SS
words: they?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, darn right they fell down.

speaker: SS
words: Well, they couldn't have been in the ground too good, were they?

speaker: OS
words: Rotted off. (Chuckles) And then when there's a straight line if there

speaker: OS
words: was a good one, they take that one along with them. But it happened

speaker: OS
words: the anchor broke off and then that let the whole thing go. Yeah,

speaker: OS
words: they all fell down, you remember between Pullman and Colfax

speaker: OS
words: same thing happened up there. There was no telephone service long

speaker: OS
words: distance out of Lewiston for four or five days. It happens every

speaker: OS
words: place, that's when they holler about the buried cables. You don't

speaker: OS
words: have that trouble, but they have other troubles.

speaker: SS
words: What about on trouble shooting the line in figuring out where the

speaker: SS
words: trouble is? Let's say it's in the winter- did you use snowshoes to

speaker: SS
words: get around?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, not too much. I had to go up on Fix Ridge two or three times

speaker: OS
words: with 'em, but I wasn't very much good with them things. They're hard

speaker: OS
words: to walk- you ever have 'em on?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, they're funny walking. I never learned how really.

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, they're funny is right. I walked about a mile up there one

speaker: OS
words: time and that was tough. (Conversation with Mrs. Schupfer-)

speaker: OS
words: It don't Spokane, it left at twelve noon, I thinknext

speaker: OS
words: morning They, stayed overnight here and went back- and then there was

speaker: OS
words: another train come down again. They always had the two trains every

speaker: OS
words: day; one each way.

speaker: SS
words: So, Juliaetta was that- did it stop here for the night?

speaker: OS
words: It doesn't end but they al went up to Kendrick because they had a

speaker: OS
words: water tank up there and a little bit better accommodations.

speaker: SS
words: How did they turn the train around here?

speaker: OS
words: Well, it backed down, but they had a turntable in Kendrick, they just

speaker: OS
words: turned around, the engine, just hooked onto the other end. They had

speaker: OS
words: a turntable at Kendrick- I don't suppose you ever seen any.

speaker: SS
words: No.

speaker: OS
words: They put the engine on a rig and then it-a handle on each end and tur

speaker: OS
words: ned it around and balanced it on there. But they had another way.

speaker: OS
words: Down at Arrow was the first one, it was a Y. They'd go out on one

speaker: OS
words: track, back up, go the other direction and that way turn the whole

speaker: OS
words: thing around.

speaker: SS
words: So, it backed down into Juliaetta?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, they backed down into Juliaetta. They're doing the same thing

speaker: OS
words: now with the freight.

speaker: SS
words: I'm surprised they just didn't put the turntable down in Juliaetta

speaker: SS
words: so it could come—

speaker: OS
words: Oh, there was kind of a, I don't know, politics went on in Juliaetta.

speaker: OS
words: Juliaetta didn't give the right kind of right-of-way and in Kendrick

speaker: OS
words: they did, so they kind of made Kendrick the end. And then Kendrick

speaker: OS
words: is right the head of that Troy grade. See, that was a bad grade, for

speaker: OS
words: them days.anyway.

speaker: SS
words: I'd heard something about one time there was a real bad accident

speaker: SS
words: coming down to Kendrick.

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, there was a bunchleft Troy there, two engines on and a bunch

speaker: OS
words: of railroad rails, I don't know where they was going with 'em but

speaker: OS
words: they couldn't hold 'em comingto Kendrick and the brakeman on the back

speaker: OS
words: end uncoupled the caboose and he stopped that and another fellow tried

speaker: OS
words: to uncouple the rails, but the goldarn thing flopped over in the creek

speaker: OS
words: and killed him. Then got down to Kendrick and the

speaker: OS
words: Kkilled four of em.

speaker: SS
words: Well, was the brakeman in the caboose at fault?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, there's always someone in the caboose.

speaker: SS
words: But what I mean is- should he have cut the caboose loose? Did that

speaker: SS
words: make it worse?

speaker: OS
words: Yeah, he cut the caboose loose, that's the only thing that didn't

speaker: OS
words: wreck, as far as I can remember. The rest of it was all wrecked.

speaker: OS
words: And there was another time that there was several carloads of hogs-

speaker: OS
words: and as they pulled up about Kendrick they broke loose, they come down

speaker: OS
words: here backwards. And Herman and I was downtown and we seen the darn

speaker: OS
words: thing going by at a pretty good speed. far behind was the engine

speaker: OS
words: and I guess they had a freight train out on the track somewhere

speaker: OS
words: between here and Arrow and they run into that. Smashed all the cabooses

speaker: OS
words: but they never hurt a pig.

speaker: SS
words: You mean, it came clear down through Juliaetta, all the way down to

speaker: SS
words: Arrow?

speaker: OS
words: Did it what?

speaker: SS
words: Went all the way down to Arrow before they hit?

speaker: OS
words: Oh, yeah. Pretty near the same thing happened the other day. They

speaker: OS
words: was loading a car up there at Kendrick and it was shaking a little bit

speaker: OS
words: and finally the darn thing took off all by itself and started down

speaker: OS
words: the track on the siding and it got down there; there's a

speaker: OS
words: I might have showed you one of those before-

speaker: SS
words: A Derail?

speaker: SS
words: derail

speaker: OS
words: Yes, a you know what that is, don't you? It's a car comes

speaker: OS
words: down and it runs over the track- well, they had the car setting there

speaker: OS
words: about four or five days before they got it back on.

speaker: SS
words: Why is it that this is such a rough area for trains? Did you say

speaker: SS
words: it's harder than most?

speaker: OS
words: The company wanted the right-of-way for nothing, and people wasn't

speaker: OS
words: quite willing to do it that way. And it seems like Juliaetta wouldn't

speaker: OS
words: give it to 'em and Kendrick did. And Kendrick is named after a rail

speaker: OS
words: road engineer, so they kind of favored Kendrick. And Kendrick was

speaker: OS
words: a little bit. They had about four or five rigs coming in

speaker: OS
words: there for freight; Juliaetta didn't have that muchbut

speaker: OS
words: when they got to Juliaetta- it seems that there are two stories

speaker: OS
words: there. The Indian Reservation, they couldn't cross it down here,

speaker: OS
words: Juliaetta permit- another one I think is, they run out of money.

speaker: OS
words: They stopped in Juliaetta for four years.

speaker: SS
words: Juliaetta had mostly- it was Potlatch Ridge and Fix Ridge and was

speaker: SS
words: there any other ridges that Juliaetta had?

speaker: OS
words: This American Ridge up here, butA go back very far, that

speaker: OS
words: went to Kendrick. Kendrick had Potlatch Ridge and LelandSouthwick

speaker: OS
words: and Bear Ridge and Texas Ridge and Little Bear Ridge and American

speaker: OS
words: Ridge. They had quite a little more there. I think you could find

speaker: OS
words: that somewheres in the history book, just why that was- I don't know
